I myself have a gas fire on 24/7. 20hrs a day is just a blue flame. It heats the chimney stack and both radiate heat. I'm putting heat into a mass. This also has an effect on the room above with the chimney running though it. So no I cannot believe how warm this gets as no measure of it was provided.

author: @WhiskeyBU2
text:
While the video briefly mentions running a "little bit of safety" by putting the stove in a surround (1:42), it does not detail the significant dangers associated with this type of homemade heater.

Based on external information, homemade methanol heaters pose extreme dangers due to several factors:

Fire and Explosion Hazards:
Invisible Flames: Methanol burns with a clear, often invisible flame, making it difficult to detect and increasing the risk of accidental burns.
Refilling Risk: Pouring methanol into a still-warm heater can cause a flashback, leading to a fireball and severe burns.
Lack of Safety Features: Unlike commercial products, homemade heaters lack essential safety features like mechanisms to prevent fuel spillage or ensure the unit is cool before refilling.
Health and Poisoning Hazards:
Extreme Toxicity: Methanol is highly poisonous. Ingestion of even small amounts can be fatal for adults, and contact or vapor inhalation can be dangerous.
Vision Loss and Organ Damage: Exposure to methanol can cause permanent vision loss, organ damage (including liver and kidneys), and neurological damage.
Vapor Inhalation: Methanol vapors are heavier than air and can form dangerous clouds, especially during refilling, posing an inhalation and ignition risk.
Other Risks:
Using a homemade heater may void your insurance coverage, as it is likely considered an unsafe heating device.
Homemade units might introduce other airborne contaminants.

Therefore, constructing and using such a device carries significant risks to safety and health.

replies.text: ​​@WhiskeyBU2 I've read some comments on his videos where people said that his ideas kept them warm enough to make it through the winter. Looking at some of the other comments you tend to leave, it seems like you just have a problem with people saying nice things about others or trying to contribute something meaningful to a conversation. What a sad life you must lead, dude. Fear not, I'm sure there will be no dramatics when you kick the bucket, if anyone can be bothered to feel sad at all for such an enormous loss in the first place. I mean, come on dude, God forbid anyone try to offer respect for the dead. Your conduct is, quite frankly, disgusting. Just like my old neighbor, nobody was sad when he bit it. His wife's adult children actually started to visit again, the neighborhood kids felt safe walking on that side of the street for once, and nobody had much to say about Eddie except how relieved they were to not deal with his constant crappy attitude. Congratulations on your contributions to humanity, friend. I'm sure we're all richer for knowing you, even if only for the fact that it gives us the opportunity to grow in charity. You're really out here saving the world one comment at a time. Your legacy will never be forgotten... until the very first opportunity to do so. Being a misanthrope must be so sad and exhausting, and I'm sorry you suffer that way. I hope the universe will bring you a swift solution and end to your pain. Good day and godspeed.

author: @ozwzrd
text:
Insurance companies will not pay out if an un-certified "stove" or the like is found in a post-conflagration inspection... whether it was in use or not. 

I subscribe to this channel and love the info it provides. But there needs to be a caveat when dealing with the liabilities of DIY projects. 

For safety/security's sake you can always (and should) consult with local fire authorities .

author: @k.sullivan6303
text: DO NOT USE THIS HEATER IN YOUR HOME! It is very dangerous. Methanol, Natural Gas, and Propane all burn clean as long as there is complete combustion. This appliance gives off a lot of CO2 which displaces the oxygen in the living quarters, and then the appliance cant have total complete combustion and at that point starts producing carbon monoxide, which further depletes the oxygen in the room and things just keep getting worse. To lessen the amount of CO2 in the combustion cycle, you can open a window, thus allowing fresh air into the room. However, at that point you will loose almost all of the heat that the appliance creates. Many of these channels say nothing about the carbon monoxide risk, and this guy clearly did not mention the very extremely dangerous hazard.

author: @k.sullivan6303
text: How many people died from doing what this channel suggests? I work in the heating and air conditioning business. This heater is burning a fossil fuel. The channel didn't mention a damn thing about carbon monoxide risks which are huge with this type of heater.

author: @Bluuri-Aeros
text:
I am tying my shoelaces to go out to my converted shuttle bus (Go-Go Danger Bus!) to build this inspiring design!  I'm wondering about a couple things, though: would this work well with a paraffin or oil fuel? More importantly to my needs, does anyone' have experience with adding venting to a heater like this? Such as, ideally, a "double vented chimney", if that is the correct terminology? The exhaust moving up and through the ceiling or bent 90° and through the wall, but also having the exhaust fumes exiting a pipe within another,  larger diameter pipe in whichbthe outer, larger pipe's air (heated by the inner pipe's hot, exiting exhaust) would be blown/directed via a small blower into the room being heated. 
I'd also think about a fresh air intakento feed the fire, before being exhausted out the chimney.
I know these aren't unique, and would increase the safety of the heater... I'm just unsure if all this would tie/work together in such a small heating device.
I'm thinking ill be warm this winter, now having a Little Buddyb propane heater, a propane stove AND oven, a Chinese diesel heater (5k,) several 12v electric blankets, my slightly overweight Boston Terrier,  Lola, thermal long underwear,  a book of matches, memories of my wild,  wild younger days and possibly now this!  When you're hot, you're hot 😅

text:
Efficiant heating with that heater and I love it, and love efficiency. 😆💙 A tip for hot water bottles: 
Chuck them away and fill up a 1.25 liter plastic soda bottle with tap water (DON'T use boiled water). Use tap water only! And the circular cross section of a soda bottle has less surface area to volume ratio. And thus retains heat for much longer. No boiling water, simply use tap hot water, no need for a jacket (tap water won't burn cos it's not as hot) yet retains heat for LONGER than a regular hot water bottle with boiled water! How's that for efficiency?! 😁 And you can have multiple bottles for much cheaper than regular hot water bottles (and cheaper hot water). And better for the planet by not boiling water. The list goes on. Do a video on it! 😮😅🎉

author: @joemelton81
text: Neat heater!!!  This is my second winter living without grid electricity and I have been heating with experimental heaters. I have been playing with candles, alcohol stoves, oil lamps and kerosene. I love the mantle and reflector design. I intend to try it next. I'm currently using a big aluminum air cooled cylinder head suspended just above an oil lamp. It gets hot and the aluminum cooling fins dissipate the heat into the room. It's not perfect but it is cheap and runs all night. The idea came from the terracotta candle heaters and the fact that I have condemned cylinders laying around everywhere. The next improvement is going to be a fan for circulation.

Just made mine and it works great.  I did it using the hole in the top and i use a washer with bolt to cover it.  I like this version better than the 1746 design because this is easier to snuff out.  Radiates heat really well but i go through rubbing alcohol rather quickly.

I found the carbon felt on amazon as well as the wire mesh.  Used 8 inch metal ducting at Lowes and cut it down from 24" to 18 and used 2 cake tins from dollar tree

author: @Screamingtut
text: hey Bob, Out of curiosity, I was wondering how hot does it really get? love the comedy you put into your videos. I'm watching this video on my "39th" Birthday (With that is 31 years' worth of Really Hard Knock Experience as an Electronic/Computer Engineer). What is funny is that Mentally, I feel like a 45-year-old but the Body feels like 135 years old. (I just turned 70, BTW). I would have loved to have you as a teacher in school. Several of my Teachers and professors in important classes had these very Mono-Tone voices. One was in My advanced math class, In some of my Mechanical Design Engineer Classes, I was doing Calculus to calculate to get the max stress on a supporting Beam (I Beams) in the structure I started to take Mechanical Design (because I was going to be an Architect)  for 3 years in HS (two years were drafting and the last year classes were 98% Theory), and when I was about to I graduated in 1972  the building market crashed really hard in NYC. I had a job already lined up at a very large  Architectural firm as a junior draftsman (I had worked the summer (3 months) before full-time.) Then about 3 weeks before was to graduate the owner called and said he could not hire me because of the crash he even said he had let go 3 of his Engineers.

author: @harrymills2770
text:
I love this idea.  Keep coming back to it.  I want to use it for my mini-camper, but I want to also maximize the air exchange, to defeat condensation, which is bad in those little boxes.  If I put this heater (or a smaller one) in a box with lots of glass in it, with an air intake in the front, like the front damper on any wood  stove.  Then using 1/2-inch screws for spacing, stick the 2.5-inch chimney pipe inside a 3.5-inch diameter section of stove pipe.  However much the alcohol burner breathes, it's exhaling up the chimney.  Use that draft to bring in fresh air from outside that gets heated coming in through the space between the 3.5" and the 2.5" pipes.

That would guarantee the air would get turned over and I wouldn't be damp in the morning.

My Mini-camper has doors on either side, so I can make a plywood or composite insert for one of the door openings and cut a hole for the double-walled pipe I'll have for the horizontal stretch.  I don't want the double wall part to be vertical, because I don't want convection to fight the convection cell I'm using to power the air circulation.  But I bet if I make the horizontal stretch 8 or 10 inches, the incoming air will be warmed up by the hot chimney running up the middle.  Maybe instead of screws as spacers, I should add longitudinal fins to the 2.5" pipe in the center.  Conduct more heat to the fresh air passing over it.  But I think that would create a little blower bringing fresh air across the top of the stove.  The stove would be breathing air from the bottom of the camper.  The fresh air coming in would be heated by the chimney on its horizontal journey through the plywood insert.  


Winnerwell puts out a triple-walled pipe section for going through walls.  But I don't think it's made to bring fresh air in.  Anyway, I'd like to do a simple version, and then maybe add a reflector to the back two sides of the stove.  facing the corner the stove would be in.  It's easy to cut holes in those old ammo cans and put windows in 'em and I bought some window glass for wood stoves that's tempered and nice and thick.

The one thing I didn't like about RM-s's heater was it was burning O2 and not helping the air exchange.   I really wanted it in an airtight box and help the space breathe, by bringing in warm fresh air.

author: @wendy_says_so
text:
Loved this project - so much so I've started experimenting with variations. Having done that I have a couple of recommendations: 
First,  that "small hole" on the inner can - my first attempt at the "stove" part blew the inside can clear into the air about 10 minutes after I started it with about 1/3 full with alcohol. People - if you try this project, Make sure the hole is either very, very small or much larger to prevent potential rocket-cans! A large hole will evac the pressure. You can use a rolled up piece of the carbon felt to act as a firestop in the hole - that blowing off is a lot safer than the can! 
Second, putting the whole heater inside the reflective surface works, but it also puts the fuel in the reflective path. In a longer burn on mine, I found the outside can to be difficult to touch, and the alcohol started to boil a bit, resulting in a more aggressive burn and shorter burn time. I'm planning on putting the heater into a cut down coffee-can (i.e., larger than the bean can)  and filling the coffee can with water to help keep the fuel cool. Plus I plan on cutting some aluminum to act as a heat shield, making it about the diameter of my reflector, and securing it  around the outside can and under the mantle. This will direct the heat from the mantle away from the base, helping to keep the alcohol even cooler. 
I also modified the mantle design - mine mantle to the outside can (not the inside) and the top piece of can, securing them together and helping to prevent any blown off cans. I also used a light gauge steel tube brazed between the top piece of can and the center of the small can with a hole cut in both, so I can use it to fuel the lower can. This makes the whole assembly one piece. If I have to replace the wick material, I can do that by removing the screws at the bottom of the mantle, pulling the two pieces apart.  Regarding my reflector, mine will sit inside a five gallon steel can with a fairly wide opening cut in the side of the steel can about 3 inches from the bottom. That way, in the case of the unit spilling alcohol, any flammable liquid is contained in the heater itself, preventing a bigger fire from occurring. 
All in all, sir, thank you for the inspiration!

author: @robertphillips93
text:
As the owner of a conical metallic windscreen/pot support for a methanol camping stove, the idea of leveraging this equipment to use your idea is too attractive. The screen is about 8 inches high, with a cutout section maybe 4 x 4 to accommodate the nested pot handles -- or to radiate the IR from a heater!

A small tomato paste can inside a slightly shorter and wider veggie can with 6mm welders felt wicking -- and voila, a tiny car camper heater! Still experimenting with overall height and fuel reservoir size. The stainless mantle is easily capped by the crimped top of the bigger can, and the fuel reservoir can be stabilized with an epoxied fender washer or 6mm steel square of suitable size. The whole unit will sit in a large stainless bowl secured from disturbance.

text:
I’ve made one of these with great anticipation. A great way to heat the ice fishing tent. However I’ve run into a few issues. 

1. The fumes from the methyl hydrate are terrible. Burn my nostrils. I’m not sure I’m getting a complete burn

2. It seems to only burn the raw fuel and not the vapours. I’ve made penny stoves before and they are most efficient when you get them to bloom. Trangia stoves are a great commercial example. 

Desperately want this to work and I’m looking for some insight into what I could do to fix these issues.

author: @hardyspicer2163
text:
Great video thanks, very educational.

I have been inspired to make two versions using methanol as the fuel.

Firstly, an off the shelf pocket meths stove with a 4" tall 1mm mesh mantle jubilee clipped around the stoves screw thread.   I have topped the mantle with the lid of a jam jar.  It works great either on its own or underneath one of the off the shelf BS-150 Outdoor Cosy heater caps from video 1786.  When the BS-150 is used, I can boil a camping kettle in minutes.

Second version is a duplicate of the tin can burner in this video, but instead of making the surround stand in this video I have simply placed it inside an existing old cast iron chiminea.  As the chimney flue is not needed I have just put a cake tin on top and it keeps my tin mug of coffee warm as well as heating me in the garage.

I wouldn't have considered doing these if it wasn't for your videos.  So I'd either be using a gas camping stove or installing a wood burner chimney.  I'd now be happy to take the chiminea indoors now if our central heating goes down.  Thanks again.

author: @kellyvcraig
text:
HMMMMM

A friend is a scrapper and has some major large stainless steel tubes. He's hard pressed to figure out what he wants to do with them.  AND, orchardists use stainless steel mesh screens to filter water into their irrigation circles (see Note). Said another way, that sounds like a whole lot of potential for a system on steroids.

Oh, and the guy does a lot of biodiesel, so gets his methane in 55 gallon drums.

NOTE: Circles are irrigation systems consisting of a single pipe stretching across half a, for example, 100 acre field. The pipe is supported by towers about 90 feet apart. Each tower has wheels driven by electricity and is moved when micro switches are activated.

ALL THAT ASIDE, I'd love to know, in a remote ballpark, how many BTU's we'd see out of a half can run.

replies.author: @chalf03
replies.text:
Why not use ethanol instead of methanol? Ethanol would generate more heat. Be careful when burning anything, because you can get deadly carbon monoxide poisoning. Burn things outside, get a carbon monoxide detector, if you are going to burn inside.

Methanol:
57,250 BTU/gal
8,639   BTU/lb

Ethanol:
76,330 BTU/gal
11,587  BTU/lb

Source: ESSOM heating value chart

author: @brotherlove100
text: hypothetical question: would it make sense to double up on the mantle?  if heat capture is the goal, would doubling the medium also double the heat output?  I was thinking about a cm gap b/w the two

replies.author: @kebman
replies.text: Most likely no. There is no added effect from the burned medium. The mantle glows infrared, and the radiation is then mirrored. You can't "add" any more effect to that than what is already in the fuel. But you _can_ direct it.

I just bought a proffesionally made keroscene heater.  A 3kw one costs me £1-£2 quid a day, to have it running constantly for 14 hours.  And at 3KW it keeps it toastie believe and also it has safety features such as a carbon monoxide detector, it turns off if its knocked, it turns of if its not burning properly and stops it releasing toxic fumes.

Oh and also methylated spirits contains a denaturant, i WOULD NOT LIKE TO BE BREATHING THE TOXIC FUMES left over from it when its burned alongside the methylated spirit.  totally stupid, dangerous idea, so easy to knock over and it would go everywhere.

author: @shantz7
text:
Hello and thank you for such a great Idea for providing heat this winter. I have a serious question for you though. I have built one exactly as you did and even with filled full with ethanol, it only burns for 35 minutes. It seems to be burning hotter that the one in you video but only lasts a short while. I wanted something clean burning that I could light before going to bed and wake up with a warmer than 10c house. I live in a very small place and when I had the heater burning for 30 minutes, it did warm the place up by several degrees Can you please offer me some advise. I've invested in 60 liters of methanol and I don't want it to be all burned up in the first week..

Dahlia

Thanks again for the channel and this great heater; which obviously is something we've done wrong as we saw yours burn for an hour on only a little fuel.  We used the same cans and fuel and the 5 mm carbon wick.

Thanks again, Emma

author: @paulmolyneaux5779
text:
I thought, That's f@cking easy .
  So went out shopping for the simple parts 
Feeling jubilated .

  4 weeks later after physically searching .
  I'd located the food tins .
And a sheet of stainless steel.

Wire mesh = 0
Carbon felt =0
20 cm standard baking trays =0
Ethanol (almost = 0

Improvisation
Out of desperation .
  I compiled an Idea, using .
-2 dog food bowls .
-4 food tins .( 2 different sizes )
-3 nuts and bolts. 
-1 Stainless steel metal sheet.
(Hand and knee bent.
 into a parabola.)
- 1 small bag of quick drying cement .(to add support and weight to the base )
-  1/4 meter Kevlar .( Juggling fire touch wick,I had kicking about )
And 
-1 wire mesh office paper bin 

- Bio Ethanol.

Doesn't quite look the same as in the YT video .

Needs a bit of tweaking .

At least Now, I have heating .

Paul Molyneaux

and if I fill mine half way or more, I'm lucky if I get another ten minutes

and if you can't get 4 hours...why did you say you can ???

I made this and for safety (because I have a one eyed dog with a tendency to bounce around and knock into things) I bought my garden chimenea inside and placed it inside, the bulk of heat radiates out of the hole in the front, but the body of the chimenea also gets surprisingly warm. I'm curious to whether I could make some sort of cavity to fill with sand in the fatter round body area of the chimenea to extend the time this remains warm after the heater burns out?
Grateful for all ideas, suggestions or reasons to not bother 😁

author: @jenniferclarke3622
text: I just finished making this and I am so disappointed.  Everything was done exactly according to the video instructions.  I used carbon felt (3mm) and very fine stainless steel mesh.  To test, I used about 350 ml of methyl hydrate.  Once ignited, it burned for 30 minutes and the fumes were eye-watering.  Fortunately, it was in the garage and I was able to open the door to get rid of the smell.  The only visible difference I can see between Robert's stove and mine is that mine had a flame that burned blue and yellow.  The wick did glow red but It  didn't give out that much heat.  The only conclusion I can come to is that I made a mistake by using cans from pineapples and peaches instead of peas and carrots.  I wasted about $60.

replies.text: How did you get on with it?

replies.author: @jamiedalton2623
replies.text: @awatt  If I could post an image of it on here, I would. It looks pretty good and was an easy build, but the methanol I used set off my carbon monoxide detector almost immediately, and it didn't matter if my burner and detector were on separate floors! I bought the fuel on Amazon. I then switched to using ethanol, and that was fine, but it doesn't make the mantle glow in the same way. The Ethanol didn't set my detector off even when it was right next to the heater. This has made the reflector element of it redundant, but I wouldn't burn Methanol in it again.

replies.author: @jamiedalton2623
replies.text: @blackbulldog4897  6 months later (sorry, YT didn't notify me of your reply) I used a small sheet of stainless steel for the reflector which I polished up, bought from Amazon, and I got the felt (welders mat) from ebay.

author: @britishdroneservices9355
text: Hello Robert, or anybody else who may be able to answer this question as to where we possibly went wrong. Myself and my mate decided to build one of these and took extra precautions to be on the safe side, we followed all the instructions as per video, the only difference was that the tin with the holes around the bottom and one in the top centre that fits into the other was a bit of a tight fit, we couldn't get it to ignite easily and my mate put the lighter over the hole at the top to ignite it, it basically exploded forcing the inside tin to hit the top lid with some force, made us jump a bit but nobody was hurt in the process, we did this outside in his garden with a fire extinguisher nearby incase we needed it, fortunately we didn't need it, but why did it explode ? was it because the fitment was too tight and not enough air was getting into it, or the hole in the top could have been too big, if anybody could help it would be appreciated, also noticed quite a bit of smoke and fumes coming from it as well which would be a no no indoors. regards

replies.author: @divingsnow
replies.text: Similar thing happened to me when I was trying to understand why I was only getting half an hour burn time, as apposed to Robert's 4 hours: I put a shim between the tins. They get hot which causes the methanol in the tin to vaporise. The expansion of gasses blew the inner tin into the mantel. Luckily I had fixed the mantel to the reflector so it absorbed the impact rather than igniting my face. Found recently that the methanol when vaporising is flowing over the outer tin and igniting in the bottom tray which is a little bit alarminf. Found the best was to let it burn off/out. Don't think this heater is for me. Be safe: liquids transitioning to gas will expand dramatically and when confined will cause an explosion.

replies.text: I've built something similar to this and it is okay, but is it safe? Anytime you burn anything in the house no matter how safe they say it is, you need to be cautious, because anything can happen. The reason is you are burning ALCOHOL. If you are not in a total darkness, it will be hard for you to see its flame. That's not good if you accidentally splashed burning ALCOHOL across your living space during the day. Can you heat your WHOLE HOME with this? NOPE. If you want to heat your whole home with this, YOU NEED VERY BIG UNIT of this heater. Bigger the unit more the heat it generates to circulate throughout your home. But at the same time, the risk of burning your home goes up not to mention Carbon monoxide poisoning. In other word, you need to be watching this heater all the time while it is burning ALCOHOL. In all honesty, I would just buy myself an electric heater that you can just plug and turn on *(but your electric bill is going to go up)* or wood stove or if you have a fireplace in your house just start burning woods in your fireplace. I know some people turn all the junk mails, fliers, ads in the mail into log to burn in their wood stove. Hey its free energy. Tha's my two cents Debbee... Have a great day.🙂

author: @pacman4568
text: I did think of a way to add to the heat exchange. If a pipe was put into the reflector at an angle above the mantel, the pipe would get hot and the air with in the pipe would rise and come out of the higher part of the pipe, with cool air being replenished from the lower part of the pipe. This would greatly increase the heat exchange and eliminate some of the loss of fuel consumption.

author: @kennymonty8206
text:
And now you won't freeze while you watch your children starve to death the next time some insane politician gets a bright idea.
Sorry for the dark humor. On a more serious note, I'd be interested to know what the gaseous by products are. Are there dangerous levels of poisonous gases released? Even carbon dioxide, which is not poisonous but can displace enough oxygen, to cause problems, namely suffocation. Also, I'm curious what the combustion temps are at various points. If it's high enough, many gasses produced by combustion are flammable. It could be that a secondary combustion system could make this more efficient and safer. It would add complexity to your very simple and elegant design, however. But, we are humans, arguably capable of incredible things. The difference might be too small to make it worth the work, but it would be fun to find out. Just a thought.

replies.author: @waltershumate5777
replies.text:
You know how they say people get to the end of their lives and they "wish they had one more day"? 
You suffer from depression, so do I, but less now that I thought of this.
How would we like it if we came to the end of our Lives, begging for one more day... " oh but you already had it!?"  We spent it feeling sorry or bummed out about something that happened that was obviously beyond our control and that we are unable to fix instead of just moving on.  With a human loss, what can you say except goodbye? It can't be undone..?  What good is our sadness going to do for it?  It won't fix it or take it back, or change anything in any way.  
I personally was caught up in a matter of human loss. My sorrow won't bring them back, it didn't make any of the memories anymore precious, and what could they say from their side? I'm pretty sure it wasn't their idea to go and die!? But now that he has I'm sure he'll be just fine. That means it's for me that I cry and not for them. Maybe I'm selfish, or a little unsure of what the future will bring, or a little of both. But as long as I'm still on the right side of the grass I'm going to have to confront both. I may as well do it with my feet solidly under me and my chin Held High.

text: Fantastic content Rob, love the projects you show on your channel.  After building this one in particular, I was excited to give it a try.  It did light well and produce a good amount of heat.  However, after about 5/10 minutes using 99.5% Methanol, I was getting a burn off fume which burned my eyes and made my nose stuffy.  My carbon monoxide alarm was also going crazy.  Surely I have done something wrong, as this shouldn't happen should it?  Any advice would be much appreciated.

replies.author: @NinjaRobotics
replies.text:
I also set my carbon monoxide sensor off. Just before this I noticed that the flames had made their way into the center of the mantel, even though I used 1mm stainless steel mesh. The flames ignited the methanol evaporating out of the filling hole I made in the top of the smaller can.
If methanol (or any other fuel) burns without enough oxygen, it will give off carbon monoxide instead of carbon dioxide. And the air inside the mantel would definitely be low in oxygen, considering it is encircled by fire. So if a fire gets going inside the mantel this can easily lead to incomplete combustion and a dangerous amount of carbon monoxide. My detector measured 70ppm which upon research is enough to knock you out cold, cause brain damage, a coma or worse.
A CO sensor with a led display should be considered essential when doing anything like this indoors, as by the time most detectors go off the levels are already dangerously high.
@Andrew Cooper were you able to find a solution? One comment suggested covering the hole with a coin, though I've gone with a design more similar to the one shown in video 1738.

author: @paulbradford8240
text: You have a heater that has no safety cut out. Modern paraffin heaters have a CO2 cut out, plus a vibration sensor, so if you knock it, it will go out. Methanol is also four times the price of paraffin/kerosene. An interesting project, but not economical or safe. Modern paraffin/kerosene heaters can run for up to 19 hours on a single fill.

text:
What you have here is basically a kerosene heater.
Burning alcohol just makes the stove slightly less efficient than the Kero Sun kerosene type heaters.
My kerosene heater burns for approximately 24 hours on one fill up, and has various safety features built in. I have used it to heat a basement rec room for several years.

author: @ericberg2131
text: FYI, the main problem you are going to face in constructing this heater is getting the carbon felt. Robert doesn't really mention it here but you're going to need a very thin carbon felt on the order of 1 mm thick, perhaps 2 mm would also work. I mistakenly got a 5 mm thick piece of carbon filter and it simply does not wick, ie. the fuel doesn't climb up it very easily. Finding such carbon felt is difficult. Robert's store is out of stock with it. Ebay was selling it for $788/ for a large roll but there was nothing smaller that I found. Additionally, methanol is not exactly cheap. A quart of it cost me $20 on ebay. However, you can get it in a 5 gallon container which will run you about $25/gallon. Any thoughts on this matter from those who have built one of these heaters would greatly be appreciated. Cheers.

author: @twiglet2214
text:
No mention of the dangers of handling methanol ?   Hazards Associated with Using Methanol

Methanol is highly flammable and toxic. Direct ingestion of more than 10mL can cause permanent blindness by destruction of the optic nerve, poisoning of the central nervous system, coma and possibly death. These hazards are also true if methanol vapors are inhaled.

When handling methanol, including biofuels, it is best to avoid direct exposure as much as possible. As such, it is imperative that safety gear be worn, especially those that cover the face, eyes and skin. If working where methanol vapors are present, proper ventilation is imperative for safety.

Should methanol come into direct contact with the skin, remove any contaminated clothing and wash the affected area with soap and water for 15 minutes. If methanol comes into contact with the eyes, flush immediately with tepid water for 15 minutes and then seek qualified medical help. Lastly, if ingested, seek immediate medical attention and do not induce vomiting.

author: @KirbyLWallace
text:
So, I built VERY close to this spec.  My wick material was 3mm instead of 5mm.  And I made my mantle fit over the outside edge of the outer can, so ALL the flame was emitted inside the mantle.

I noticed that a taller mantle just wasted hot gas by not heating ALL of the mantle to glowing.  It had "cold spots" where it wasn't glowing at all.  So, I snipped a couple inches off the top, reducing the mantle height from about 8 inches to about 5 1/2 inches.  Much better.  Pretty much ALL of the mantle was glowing red hot and radiating heat.  **BUT** , 

I got NOWHERE near the fuel efficiency.  Mine went through a full 8 oz of alcohol in just ONE hour.  ???  I'm using denatured alcohol, (which is sold as actual fuel, and is rated as a higher energy density than methanol).  So, maybe burning hotter means burning twice as fast?

But no doubt about it.  Once that mantle gets glowing, this thing is radiating heat that you can feel six feet away.  And I only have the mantle done so far.  No enclosure cabinet to focus heat to the front.

Instead of the aluminum sheet, use a  26 gauge C-Vent pipe.  You can get them from any hardware store or HVAC supply store. They come in many sizes.  A 12" or bigger should work. You can also snap two smaller c-vent pipes together to make one big one. 
C-venting is heater exhaust pipe that looks like regular heat supply pipe which is 30 gauge.

replies.author: @salaciouscreations4323
replies.text: I bought an all metal electric fire and gutted the inside  for my one and added 20kg of sand in stacked and alu taped tin cans with a copper tube in the middle  around the burner and on top of the fire unit.  The sand batteries worked  they worked best with a 2 hour burn for the room to be warm for 4 hours.    Cost me about £300 for November to march in methanol and we weren't cold in the room.   Had some issues with the mantel causing monoxide alarm so I used a IKEA stainless cutlery holder as a mantel as any attempt to regulate the air  resulted in monoxide.   Also avoid covering the hole at the top as that also produces monoxide.    This year ( already bought 10 x 20ltr ) I am going to try sand as the wick material.

author: @hoss3433
text: I built one of these and I'm having a bit of a problem.  Not sure exactly what's happening. But about 10 mins ago I had a second test blow up.  Well it blew the inner can completely out of the outer can.  The first time I tried it was with a longer wick and it seemed to burn just fine. Then I shortened the wick but had a problem with the inner can floating so I vented the top of it.  And when I went to remove the mantle and can to extinguish it the can shot up a bit I had ahold of it with a pair of tongs so it didn't really go anywhere but the second test I did it without the vent in the top of the can and when I lit it it burned for a few seconds and the inner can shot out of it like a rocket riccocheted off the top cover hit me in the face and slightly set my porch on fire.  Got everything extinguished and a bit of blood cleaned up.  Now I'm curious as to where I went wrong.  I didn't seem to have any issues with the longer wick that was either level or slightly above the rim of the outer can but it seems to have developed when I shortened the wick was wondering if you had any issues like this or know what may be going on

author: @jaytc3218
text: The addition of a mantle as well as a reflector is genius not to mention the overall aesthetics of the brass feet and handle. Nicely done, Sir! I've got all the parts to build a similar-size stove as this one. I think I want to scale this up just a little bit--not a great deal. One additional thing I thought of was to add a small magnet to either the bottom of the fuel can or the floor of the lower cake tin to provide some additional stability. I will probably not use a neodymium magnet as that might be too strong of a pull on the fuel can. Of course there is the law of diminishing returns w/ all of this but I think it will be worth the effort to find out.

I think I will try that instead of the brass handles. And I will try my best not to "go crazy" with this heater as Robert admonished. I probably won't do a continuous feed line; I'm going to keep it simple!

author: @thekingtroll2
text:
Our power was out for nine day s in Ohio about 10 years ago or so. I called a sister and had her come take everything in my freezer to her house for free so it didn't thaw and spoil.  The old fashioned ice chest let me keep eggs and milk in it. I was lucky and everyday got ice free from work.  One of the things we laughed about was my use of a fondue set.  The power went out about 9pm. The next morning I got my mom and aunt to stay at my house because it always stayed pretty cool without the a/c on.  They were both chomping for a cup of coffee.  I remembered I had a fondue set.  I heated up water and made coffee and they were thrilled.   I hated up 3 cans of soup and we had that for lunch.  Luckily, I had my grill with a full tank of propane and a few extra spares in the garage.
After a few days, neighbors ran out of propane and a few said they had to drive out of town to get theirs filled. Everyone was trying to get propane and the two places in town that sold it ran completely dry.
This video shows how to make a home made heater. People never prepare themselves for any power outage or disaster. Now more than ever they need to prepare.  I am not one of those guys who hoards 1,000 dried meals a soldier might eat. I am not a "prepper" but you really must think a bid ahead.  Do you have a heat source?  A fondue set or something to heat water with sterno or candles.  Preparing now avoids panicking later. Good video.

author: @neuromercy
text:
I've been seeing videos lately from other creators showing what they call a "cyclone" rocket stove, where the gas enters the stack at the side, and causes it to swirl rapidly. As they spin the gas cou ter clockwise, I assume they are in the southern hemisphere, and we would want to encourage it to spin clockwise. 

I wondered whether this is increasing the flame path in the same length stack, and if there is better heat transfer to either air or mass heater. Perhaps even a parabolic reflector around the stack? Regardless, you seem to be best positioned of anyone I am aware if to get to the bottom of this, and whether it improves heat transfer.

author: @MissingRocket
text: Two issues. 1) a fair percentage of these homemade heaters will not operate efficiently enough to avoid producing significant amounts of carbon monoxide. 2) a small percentage will be accidentally tipped over, and an alcohol fire in the home is catastrophic. author: @smert_rashistskiy_pederacii
text: This is a good and simple burner. But you can't grow methanol on trees in your garden. You need to buy it, so heating your room depends on the price of methanol and the outside temperature. I think on such a small scale it is effective only at + 10 + 5C.

author: @ori4640
text:
🚨🚨🚨🚨🚨LPG or Propane is much better for heating compared to Methanol. The energy density of methanol is only 19.7Mj/kg and 15.6Mj/L - Propane has an energy density of 49.6Mj/Kg and 25.3Mj/L. Liter propane cost about 1.61 GBP/L and Methanol costs about 3.30 GBP/L. 
Also if the burn of Methanol is not sociometric you will breathe formaldehyde fumes and that is very dangerous. 🚨🚨🚨🚨🚨

replies.text: Generally the carbon dioxide emissions are extremely low and biofires are safe to burn indoors but it depends on the room size and burn time. If you're running it for 4 hours or so you can open the window for 3-5 mins to let fresh air enter. It's best to double check the fuel you use but in terms of design you can buy similar products on the market.

author: @f.d.6667
text: Definitely gonna steal from that concept... HOWEVER: *Aluminium is a rather poor IR reflector* (that's why cheap mirrors can't be used in astronomy). Gold / Silver would be near optimal but are a bit pricey. A good compromise would be "German Silver" (some nickel-copper alloy) or a stainless steel type with a high nickel content. 18-12 would be optimal but 18-10 type is easier to come by... for instance, you can quite "easily" buy X5CrNi18-10 (V2A) foil (0.1mm)

author: @th3n3tw0rk6
text:
COST TODAY (OCT 13 2022):    Methanol 1Ltr @99.95% = £10.20 delivered        Fine Stainless Steel Woven Wire Mesh @ 30cm x 60cm = £8.50   
 Aluminium sheet @ 500mm x 500mm (0.5mm) = £12.00        Carbon Fiber Welding Blanket Felt Graphite Felt @ 12" x 8" = £6.66 (China product 2 weeks delivery)         
x2 old food stuff tins = £1.00         Brass type knobs as feet x3 = £8.00         Brass type handle = £2.99       TOTAL EST AVERAGE  = £50.00+   

 "That's not cheap but if you can save HALF of your heating costs for just 1 month by regulating your thermostat OR even better turning off your heating in on peak times (dependant on supplier and tariff) and replace with this heater (for a single room) this heater may pay for its self in 1 month (because you have halved your existing heating by half and replaced with the heat from this unit for a month), a tip if you live in a house or apartment with 2 stories (floors) is to only use the heater on the ground floor, heat rises so indirect heat lost from above the device will settle on your ceiling and be slowly absorbed in to the floor above (especially with timber joints).

You will need a power drill, drill bit and pop rivet gun though sawn bolts could also work. I would STRONGLY advise to NEVER leave the heater alone when burning, DO NOT use it in the bedroom EVER! and if you have carpeted or under floor insulation on timber laminate use a paving slab to stand the heater on (not bricks). Thanks to Robert Murray-Smith for this video - This is how we need to be thinking and fighting back - self sufficiency topples governments and organisations plans for wanting and plotting on forcing you to be dependant on "energy crisis" hand outs, Government neglect towards the working class and poorer classes "to save the planet" also appeases the likes of the WEF's attempt at lowering your expectations, quality of life and length of your life ultimately - "you will have nothing and be happy" also appears to include your homes heating! So... to Klaus Schwab Ill leave with my amendment to your so famous quote: "Ill have a Methanol heater and be even happier".

replies.author: @robertmarsh6673
replies.text:
Sadly, the heater does not work, it gives off fumes within minutes of ignition and the room or space becomes unbreathable quite quickly. I too, thought that this as a wonderful idea that Rob had found, but sadly not. Yes, I used 100% carbon Fibre and stainless-steel mesh as Rob did, I even put the brass handle and brass feet on the casing. You need some form of chimney that leads outside, but even then, it needs to be properly tried and tested. I also found that the Methanol burns out very quickly. If you fill the tin to half of its capacity it will burn out within thirty minutes, which also makes the heater expensive to run. 
As I said before, It gives off great heat but it is not viable as a home heater.

author: @richardamullens
text:
Wikipedia gives the heat of combustion of Methanol as 725.7 kJ/mol
Molecular weight of methanol 32.04 (grams per mole)
Density of methanol 0.792 g/cc
So heat liberated by burning 5L of methanol is ((5000 * 0.792) / 32.04) * 725.7/ 3600 = 24.9 kWh

Hexamine (solid fuel) heat of combustion 30MJ/kg.  1kg -> 30,000 /3600 = 8.33 kWh

replies.author: @cowboyblacksmith
replies.text: A carbon monoxide detector would be a very good idea. I just made one and love it! I burn a 50/50 blend methane ethane ( denatured alcohol fuel from the hardware store) and surprisingly there isn’t any fumes to speak of as you might suspect beforehand. pure methane in a good combustion releases water vapor and CO2 as a by product so pretty darn safe. You'd not want to be in a very tightly sealed space and burn it since it will consume oxygen and release CO2 but it's not sooty at all lime rubbing/isopropyl alcohol is, and methane only is the cleanest burning alcohol with only one carbon atom in its chain. "Heet" car gas tank water remover in the yellow bottle is pure methane but not financially feasible to buy lots of them to use as fuel. I've heard that a car racetrack sells methane the race cars use as fuel and it's about $6.00 - $8.00 / gallon, even ask where their supplier is. I hope that’s true, it'd beat the $22.00/ gallon denatured alcohol price. Best thing going would be to make a still and produce your own ethanol (moonshine). Don't think I won’t either, lol.

author: @rayblazejko6908
text:
Simply brilliant.
I have a challenge for you.
Connect a flu of copper tube .
1 tube inside another with a sealed space of say 1 inch .
Maybe 12  inches long .
2 copper pipes in the flu .
One low and one high.
Connect an  inlet and outlet hose with running water with a shower attached. 
Fill it all with water and when hot enough a continuous flow of hot water will give you a nice hot shower with only a small battery operated pump. 
As the hot water is used and cold enters it will get hot at the exit point to the shower. 
Temperature is adjusted by how fast the water is pumping. 
Set it up in the bathroom with 20 litres of water to save on electricity . Maybe gravity feed.
Outlet for shower is the lower pipe.
What you reckon?

replies.author: @cryptickcryptick2241
replies.text: A simpler way to figure out how much heat is released is to look at the amount of fuel consumed. As the fuel is burned, the heat will be released. Methanol has about 57,000 BTU per gallon. Some fuels like diesel have about twice that amount of heat. Just taking a guess from the size of the can he is holding I suspect there is about 1 cup of fuel, maybe a little more. If one cup of fuels lasts four hours, it would burn about 8 cups in 24 hours. That would be about 1/2 gallon of fuel per day so about 28,000 BTU per day. To simplify this that is about 1,000 BTU per hour. It could easily be more. Maybe 2,000 BTU per hour.

replies.author: @arjanmuyen3684
replies.text: This is the wrong kind of thinking. You won't be cold in a cold room sitting next to a radiating heater

replies.author: @arjanmuyen3684
replies.text: @cryptickcryptick2241 wrong kind of thinking, see my comment

replies.author: @cryptickcryptick2241
replies.text: @arjanmuyen3684 While it is true a radiative heater can direct heat at an individual, and make that individual both "warmer," and make that individual "feel warmer." The amount of heat that is directed is important. It is well known in the world of physics and in the wold of heating and air conditioning how much heat a space needs.  In the United States, a normal 1500 watt 120 volt electric heater gives off 5,115 BTU. According to my calculations, the heater here gives off between 1/4 to 1/2 of the that amount of heat. One small portable electric heater is generally not considered enough heat to heat a house. Sometimes it can heat one small room in a house.

replies.author: @pattiannepascual
replies.text: I have had great success using heavy duty metal plant stands with a clear glass tabletop oil lamp underneath.A neighbor was giving away 2 plant stands. One has 3 feet and the other 4 feet. They are about 2 ft. tall 12 inch diameter on top.They have a ring around the top.i put one on the floor, screwed it to a beam so it doesn't move. two bricks flat,side by side to put the oil lamp on.Found a thin metal,12 inch diameter stockpot at a thrift shop.i put that on top of the plant stand.Because it's a thin metal pot, it gets really hot at the bottom and heats water warm enough to shower with or wash dishes.I have 2 going day and night in my tent shed hybrid house. 12x20 insulated with reflectix. The oil lamps hold around 12 ounces of k'1 kerosene, last 2 or more days without refilling.A gallon of k-1 kerosene costs around $10 at Walmart,camping section. It is the least expensive source of heat and hot water. Kerosene is safe to store and keeps good forever.I tried the buddy heaters only to find you need 3 1lb canisters of propane daily, at $5 or more each 😱 I tried the propane camping stove on life for heat but didn't do much better guzzling the cans of propane. I am very happy with the low cost oil lamp, plant stands solution for light,heat and hot water. You do have to scrape soot off the bottom of the pot every couple days. I also use this to heat up soup,canned pasta,etc.-I might try the heater in this video using k-1 kerosene. Might need adjustments but maybe can make a bigger one to not have to re-fuel for several days. If not not a hassle to fill up oil lamps every couple days.❤🙏

replies.author: @robertmarsh6673
replies.text: Yes, I too was sucked in by this man's enthusiasm, but sadly the heater is dangerous, it gives off as much unbreathable fumes as it does heat. And YES, I carried out exactly the same experiment using the same items as Rob used, 100% carbon fibre wick, stainless steel mesh, I even bolted on a brass handle and brass feet to the heater body. The heater gave off a great heat, but also gave off as much unbreathable fumes as it did heat.  I was a CORGI registered gas engineer, although now long retired, and I would not recommend you use this heater without a chimney or flue incorporated in its design to remove the products of combustion (fumes) that this heater produces. Otherwise, this heater would be dangerous to use in any confined space or room.  I think that it is back to the drawing board for Rob and this heater which is in need of a complete re-design. Frankly, to be quite honest, this heater is dangerous and should never be used.

author: @Wulfrune
text:
Hello Robert.  I like the simplistic approach to engineering you've taken, but I have one issue that I think needs to be addressed.

Safety.  This device, whilst very clever and well built is NOT safe.  It's really only going to apply to someone who can religiously monitor the burner in event of an accident.  Should there be an accident, there's nothing built in to prevent it from becoming fuel for a serious house fire.  Honestly, Robert, I think anyone who's using such a clever device indoors is asking for trouble, because, unlike most commercially available heaters out there on the market, they all have built-in safety cutouts and other measures should a catastrophe happen, sadly, your device doesn't, which in my opinion makes it too dangerous for use in a house.

That said, I like your approach to your videos and I think you can teach me things, so it's a +1 sub from me.

Thanks

Wulfrune

author: @rebelwithcause4908
text:
It is a simple heater which costs very little to build but you need to emphasize that heaters that use carbon compounds as fuel, including methanol, produce carbon monoxide when burned.

Carbon monoxide inside a closed area, like a house or a garage or shed that is not well ventilated with outside area, will kill you.

We don't need a bunch of people dying this winter because they watched a YouTube video of a guy running a methanol powered heater inside his workshop and the viewers think it is safe to do that for hours when it is NOT safe. Instead, it can easily be lethal!

replies.author: @cowboyblacksmith
replies.text: No, but the second best. I buy "Denatured alcohol fuel" by the gallon at the hardware store. It's 50-50 methane and ethane so a good fuel. I'm trying to track down (no pun intended) a car racetrack near me where they sell it by the gallon for the race cars. You can also find facing fuel dealers too. Some uses are for model airplane engines and such. Isopropyl alcohol even 91% burns too sooty so go with methanol preferably, then ethanol second best.

author: @Jollyprez
text: Old time kerosene heaters and lamps are very similar to this. The main difference between, say, the stoves and yours is that there is an asbestos wick to pick-up the kerosene. The burn is in a series of three cylinders - one outside the diameter of the wick, and two inside the wick. The result is virtually without fumes and can burn 12+ hours easily. Kerosene is cheaper than denatured alcohol here in the states - and safer to store and work with. I am replicating the stove wicks with fiberglass wicks. The heat IS adjustable, but extremely picky and doesn't react immediately to adjustments. My use-case is for a small cargo-trailer camper, and using outside air for combustion and exhaust to the outside as well.

replies.author: @tlangdon12
replies.text: Robert is using Methanol, not Ethanol. When Methanol burns it only produces Carbon Dioxide and Water. You shouldn't get any Methanol fumes in the home as the evapourating Methanol fumes are immediately burnt in the flame. Incomplete combustion of Methanol will produce Carbon Monoxide, so a Carbon Monoxide Alarm should always be used when any fuel burning stove is used to heat a room.

author: @luisderivas6005
text:
Simple...but deadly, too. Knock that heater over and you have yourself a nasty fire! 

BEWARE:  Gaseous methanol molecules can travel quite a distance. This could potentially spread fires in other places. Methanol containers can explode if they’re not sufficiently insulated or protected. When in contact with a platinum-blank catalyst, methanol can also ignite.

Prolonged exposure to methanol vapor can cause eye irritation, headaches, drowsiness, and fatigue. A person who accidentally ingests as little as 10 milligrams of methanol can become permanently blind as the optic nerve is destroyed. Ingesting 30 milligrams of methanol is probably fatal.

author: @Flashahol
text:
Both amazing design and amazingly simple! 
I have tested a few  different "household" fuels for emergency heating like rubbing alcohol, fondue fuel and some that are absolutely not safe for indoor use. 
I found that bright flames (like alcohol and acetone flames) do not produce much heat but emit very usable light but are not recommendable or indoor use (ESPECIALLY acetone!). Meanwhile blue-looking flames, like fondue fuel does, do not emit any usable light but produce a lot of heat or their density ratio and are usually safe for indoor use.
Now for the ideas: A magnet could help hold the stove in place and mesh on the whole front would help make it a tiny bit safer, especially if it was to fall face down.

replies.author: @ordinaryaverageguy76
replies.text: Might have to be careful of the magnet location, as magnets can lose magnetism if heated up too much.

replies.author: @justtinkering6713
replies.text: I think an orange flame is hotter than a blue flame, according to a chart I just saw.

replies.author: @Flashahol
replies.text: @justtinkering6713 Not sure what chart you are talking about but if you use a 20$ torch, the instructions are clear that the hottest part is the tip of the blue flame. Blue stars are hotter than yellow stars, color temperature in kelvin says blue is hotter than orange, etc. Color charts call red a warm color and blue a cold color, but that has nothing to do with flames.

replies.author: @Flashahol
replies.text: @ordinaryaverageguy76 Good point. My experience is that the bottom doesn't get that hot, although I never had one run for hours... a spacer might be required.

replies.author: @justtinkering6713
replies.text: @Flashahol my mistake, i alway used to think the blue tip was hotter till i saw the chart(s). Maybe I misunderstood their meaning

replies.author: @cowboyblacksmith
replies.text: I made one and love it! I can get "denatured alcohol fuel at my hardware store, it says fuel on the can too. Still love it though, no regrets at all. I've heard car racing tracks/fuel suppliers sell methane for the race cars cheaper by the gallon. It it’s true I could get a gallon for maybe $6.00 - $7.00 it would be a game changer and I'd burn that sucker all day long. I have a wood pellet stove and currently at $7.00 / 40 pound bag that goes all day it's cheaper by far. I'll figure it out though, for such a small thing it does a big job well. 👍

author: @ColinWatters
text: Nice heater but before everyone thinks this might reduce their energy bill this winter I thought I would work out the running costs. Some back of an envelope maths... Energy density of methanol is about 18MJ/L.  18MJ is about 5kWh. If you have to buy it I think Methanol cost about £2/L. That makes the running cost about 200p/5  = 40p per kWH. I think this a bit more than the current government price guarantee of 34p per kWH for electricity.  It also assumes the heater is 100% efficient. Next to work out the power output... So let's say 1/3rd of a litre lasts 4 hours. That's 6MJ in 4 hours. That means the output is around 400W.

replies.author: @limmoblack
replies.text: @ColinWatters Cheers Col, but i could afford to leave heating on 24/7, but it's families(and we're dragging in the lower middle classes here too) who are being punished by the greed of energy companies, and basically pension funds.

replies.author: @limmoblack

replies.author: @ColinWatters
replies.text: @limmoblack Ive got to agree with you there. I wish the government would extend the windfall profits tax like they are in Europe. Hopefully we won't have to repay the borrowing until inflation has eroded its value a bit. Depends if inflation is higher or lower than interest rates I guess. About the stove.. I did say it was a back of the envelope calc. The running cost could be a lot higher. Its not obvious how efficient would this stove be? Perhaps quite high in a sealed room as all the water vapour in the combustion products will likely condense out. Perhaps not so high if you need extra ventilation to let the water vapour and CO2 out and oxygen back in.

text: Thanks so much for this Robert. I caught your last video and was on the phone discussing furthering the design just last night... but I find you have done it here! I like this: it doesn't look too shabby and is probably an hour shopping for bits in the local hardware shop and and about £20 in parts and fuel. If you're right about the burn time, I'm guessing about 250ml for 5 hours so, if my maths is right, about 15 pence an hour in fuel or maybe £1 a day if you run it all the time. I'm going to try this as an alternative to the log burner, sat on top of it. I'll bung the log burner fan on top as well to assist pushing any convected heat into the room and we shall see if it makes a difference! Thanks again :)

replies.author: @druidjuicer636
replies.text: @Hurc7495 Maybe. Bear in mind Bioethanol is of a higher order of cost again to methanol. I think  something like 15p an hour is a reasonable level of cost IF it works for the application i.e. focussed heating in a single small room for comfort. Heating the whole room with gas or wood by convection is inherently more demanding and so will definitely be more expensive. My question is can it inexpensively and without much effort achieve a passable level of comfort (not luxuriousness) in the space I have in mind over Winter? We shall see!

text: Absolutely brilliant. Just curiosity here, what would happen if you switched the stainless steel mesh for a carbon felt mesh mantle?

replies.author: @theTeslaFalcon
replies.text:
Carbon is heat absorbing and resistant. I've been looking for the carbon felt wicks on Amazon, but most "carbon felt" blankets are sold as heat absorbing welding blankets for fire prevention from sparks, etc. Steel gets HOT and glows with the heat (radiates). 
So a carbon felt "mesh" wouldn't do anything useful. At best, it would keep the heat from getting out vs radiating out the heat in the burning fuel into the surrounding area. Lay the carbon felt blanket on the floor / table to reduce the chance of spillover / accidental fire.

text: How toxic are the fumes? Would you need a flue or chimney if running a heater of that size in a one bedroom flat for instance?

replies.author: @DFPercush
replies.text: Just CO2 if you're burning alcohol. It can start to become toxic at 10,000 ppm, iirc, but should really be below 2500 for generally feeling healthy. A carbon monoxide detector is a good idea with any fuel burner. Theoretically an alcohol burner shouldn't be producing CO, as long as the flame is blue and clear. But it will consume the oxygen in the room. You need some kind of ventilation for any open flame device. I would suggest an outside air intake to supply the flame, that way it heats the cold air as it comes in. If you run a small fan on that intake you can fine tune the amount of air flow and also pressurize the building so you're not getting drafts introducing more cold air.

Just ensure that your candle burns smooth by cutting out any airdraft. I know how it must sound, pertaining to using a candle as a solar source, but do try it out as it is not complexed or expensive to do. It is the same capability if you arange 3 of those 5 volt solar panels around a oillamp. The round part of the glass in the bottom yields the most energy.

author: @frankbush333
text: I have used alcohol stoves for ages, the claimed burn time is nonsense you might get 30 min to 1 hour tops. 2oz of fuel normally gives 12-14 min, enough heat to boil 2 cups of water heating an entire room for 4 hours is a stretch for sure.

replies.author: @limmoblack

replies.author: @frankbush333
replies.text: Nothing in the build alters the speed of fuel consumption, I will build one out just to see the heat it throws but now matter how you go about it 6oz of fuel only has so many therms of energy capacity. It needs a fuel feed line from a multi litre tank to be practical.

replies.author: @limmoblack
replies.text: @frankbush333 Would be interesting to see what heat s thrown out of both.

replies.author: @frankbush333
replies.text: Ethanol has 76,330 Btu/gallon you need about 5,000 btu per hour on average for a 10x10 ft space. A simple formula to determine your heating needs is: (desired temperature change in f) x (cubic feet of space) x . 133 = BTUs needed per hour. Isopropyl Alcohol has the highest heat output (12,960 Btu/lb.), burns hot, but dirty. Alcohol has roughly 656.25 Btu's per ounce. Using alcohol on average you would need to burn around 9 oz per hour to heat an average 10x10 space. Your entire setup needs to be scaled up to roughly the size of paint cans to get enough btu output to heat an average 10 x 10 ft room. This trick may work in warming a small tent a few degrees but then air quality becomes a factor.

 Kero is a bit cheaper than  Diesel but i suspect diesel and Kero stink too much for indoors heating use  ,
 Bio fuel rape seed or sunflower or Soya are about 30% more costly than diesel but might work in not making a stinky fuel ????. 
A continious feed Butane burner with a  similar simplicity would be good as that costs about € 4 euros a kilo and is similar to diesel in heat out put . The iron oxide seemd to be good idea  but need a system to use it

replies.author: @civicrisenz
replies.text: @limmoblack Well, Robert has one right there so he can show. I will build one and found felted welding material here in NZ. When I was in school in South Africa there was a major incident. Another local school have a march in town once a year and the kids were always carrying kerosene torches. They ran out of hesseen material so the teachers just poorest kerosene into the containers. Bad idea. The first spillage caused a domino effect. Kids ended up being very badly burned. (Early 90s) since then I'm always extra cautious with any volatile products in containers. I always close the lid and never ever let it sit open for one second. My kids know that rule....even with the milk bottle on the breakfast table. Just need someone to tip it over and a big mess. BTW I have been following Robert Since day 1. I remember people helping him to buy a new Webcam. Time flies.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

replies.author: @veeanon7231
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering yeah that's as I thought also you would lose most of the heat ! How would you go about heating a room with simple/ basic home made stuff like this without filling the room with suffocating gas? best I can come up with is heating water and or a soild heat exchanger out side the room (say another more ventilated room) but then this idea would just be reverse engineering a modern boiler / central heating system so might just as well cut the pipes to the radiators and JerryRig my own multifuel boiler but at this hypothetical stage it would be easier to just knock out the bricked up fire place pmsl ...but then again your left with a big hole sucking out all the heat from the room any ideas ?

replies.author: @DFPercush
replies.text: How about this: Instead of worrying about the exhaust, control the intake. Get one of those dryer vent ducts and run it from a window to supply air to the heater. That way you're heating the cold air as it comes in. You could possibly use a PC case fan to control the flow rate, or a third can to enclose the air supply. Test it by getting a stick of incense and see which way the smoke blows. It's eventually going to blow out of the heater of course, but you want to make sure the air that's going in to the base of the flame is coming from the outside supply and not the rest of the room. Not tested, just a thought.

replies.author: @daniellow426
replies.text:
Your home breaths. and if you vented a flue to the outside you can take the heat from the flue before it makes it out. I was thinking of simple radiator finns on the flue and maybe a second aluminium sheet spaced to allow air to rise behind the reflector.
There are a ton of ways to cheaply take heat from an object while allowing the air and co2 to pass outside.
 * as with anything that burns get a CO detector and install it close to  your floor (follow the directions). CO2 can kill you, CO will kill you.

replies.author: @mike289homebuilt5
replies.text: Simply put,   large furnaces have either a heat exchanger or their own seperate air source so they pull Air from the outside and half the heat goes up the chimney which is important.    As someone has mentioned regulate the airflow with vents that partially close.   As a math problem on paper i have tried to design the ultimate heating system and it is a monstrosity that looks unpractical;   practical designs are already available

author: @andreaharris116
text: Is it safe to use methanol?  Is it safe to use methanol in small spaces?  Is the burning of the methanol toxic?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I am sorry if this seems rude Andrea and I apologise if it does - but I have already answered this question 10 times already in the comments section - please read up on methanol burners, or glance at the comments -  this is a question that is just so easy to answer for yourself - methanol burners are used in boats - a lot!!!! you should never use a burner without a CO monitor and it doesn't matter what kind of burner it is - gas cooker, boiler, wood fire etc etc etc .... I don't understand what you mean by toxic the burner produces CO2 and water - I guess CO2 can be considered toxic - but then so can water - there is a thing called water poisoning

author: @C-M-E
text:
This would be MAGNIFICIENT for emergency heating, and better yet with a few bricks of limestone behind it, you'd have a very serviceable radiant heater! I could see one being in the fireplace this winter for the inevitable power outage. Once you get it up to temp, provided you have somewhere for the exhaust to leave your domicile (a flue for instance, cough), waste oil would be another contender. I save all my oil from oil changes to power my forge, but this would be an excellent stand-in when I'd rather save my readily burnable fuels for generators or whatnot. 
Solid home run, Robert! 👍

replies.author: @Paxmax
replies.text: I'd think CO2 might be the larger foe. Just make sure the room isn't small and completely sealed. Atmosphere contains 400ppm CO2, and at 10000ppm will the effect really become noticeable (even if there is plenty of oxygen). Would be terrible to feel winded just sitting down, get a headache and feel foggy brained. I'd definitely stay off the floor as CO2 is heavy and could pool up. Leaving a door with a small crack should do the trick, as it allows CO2 to trickle out and fresh air to come in.
